chunk_summary: Guidelines for managing severe anemia in SAM focus on transfusion thresholds, monitoring for overload, and avoiding diuretics for nutritional edema, with specific protocols for high-risk cases.
learning_objectives: Understand transfusion thresholds for severe anemia in SAM patients.
Learn to monitor for transfusion-associated circulatory overload and manage complications.
Differentiate between diuretic use for transfusion overload and nutritional edema.
Recognize the importance of early transfusion in suspected shock with anemia.
key_facts: Transfusion is indicated for hemoglobin <4 g/dL or 4–6 g/dL with respiratory distress.
Use 10 mL/kg of packed red cells or fresh whole blood infused over 3 hours.
Monitor pulse, respiratory rate, perfusion, and liver size during transfusion.
Signs of overload include worsening tachypnea, rising pulse, hepatomegaly, or crackles.
Diuretics like furosemide are only for transfusion-associated overload, not nutritional edema.
Indian guidance prioritizes early transfusion over repeated fluid boluses in suspected shock.
reference_quote: Indian training materials define transfusion triggers as hemoglobin <4 g/dL (or PCV <12%), or respiratory distress when hemoglobin is 4–6 g/dL.
document_key_topics: Monitoring and prevention of transfusion-associated overload
Early transfusion in shock with anemia
